pattern_recognition: Exponentiation, specifically dealing with powers and handling both positive and negative exponents. Signals include the need for efficient computation and handling of edge cases like zero and negative exponents.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Multiply x by itself n times to get the result.
approaches.algorithm: Initialize result as 1. For n times, multiply result by x. If n is negative, take the reciprocal of the result.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use the method of exponentiation by squaring to reduce the number of multiplications needed.
approaches.algorithm: If n is 0, return 1. If n is negative, compute pow(1/x, -n). For positive n, if n is even, return pow(x*x, n/2); if odd, return x * pow(x, n-1).

dry_run: For example, to compute pow(2, 10): Using the brute force approach, we multiply 2 by itself 10 times to get 1024. Using the optimal approach, we can square 2 to get 4, then square 4 to get 16, square 16 to get 256, and finally square 256 to get 1024, achieving the result with fewer multiplications.
edge_cases: When x is 0 and n is positive, the result is 0. If n is 0, the result is 1 regardless of x (except when x is also 0). If n is negative, the result is 1/x^(-n). Large inputs may lead to overflow, especially with large values of x and n.
common_pitfalls: 1) Forgetting to handle the case when n is 0, which should always return 1. 2) Not considering negative exponents, which require taking the reciprocal. 3) Failing to optimize for even and odd exponents, leading to unnecessary multiplications.
